front: Institution: British party that replaced the Liberal Party as the main centre-left party, historic rival of the Conservative Party
Institution: this party's historic heartland is Merthyr Tydfil and the South Wales valleys

Event: gradualist socialist organization named for a Punic War general known as "the Delayer"; members included H.G. Wells and George Bernard Shaw; helped found the Labour Party via the 1900 Labour Representation Committee
work: Founding: The Fabian Society & the Labour Representation Committee (1900-1906)

front: Institution: elected MP for Merthyr Tydfil in 1900, this politician became this party's first leader after its formal founding six years later
work: Founding: The Fabian Society & the Labour Representation Committee (1900-1906)

front: Event: Ramsay MacDonald led this party to its first prime ministry after defeating Conservative Stanley Baldwin in the 1923 election
Event: this party's first (1924) government fell after its leader halted the prosecution of Communist newspaper editor J.R. Campbell
Document: forged document purportedly written by the president of the Communist International, pressing sympathetic Labour forces to back an Anglo-Soviet Treaty; discredited its 1924 government
Event: in 1931, its leader Ramsay MacDonald was expelled after forming a coalition "National Government" with Stanley Baldwin's Conservatives, having faced the Invergordon Mutiny
Figure: succeeded Ramsay MacDonald as Labour leader in 1931, but lost 235 seats including his own of Burnley, and was succeeded by George Lansbury
Figure: resigned from the Labour government after his namesake Memorandum was rejected by the Chancellor in 1930, then formed the New Party and lost in Stoke-on-Trent in 1931
Figure: Labour Chancellor of the Exchequer under MacDonald who used orthodox, Gladstonian tactics against the Great Depression, refusing deficit spending against Keynes's urging
Event: this party took power from Winston Churchill's Conservatives in 1945 under Clement Attlee
work: The Attlee Government & the Welfare State (1945-1951)

front: Figure: Attlee's Foreign Secretary was Ernest Bevin, and his Minister of Health was this politician, spelled "B-E-V-A-N"
work: The Attlee Government & the Welfare State (1945-1951)

front: Event: this party created the National Health Service during Clement Attlee's administration
work: The Attlee Government & the Welfare State (1945-1951)

front: Document: 1942 report that identified the "five great evils" of want, disease, ignorance, squalor, and idleness, leading to the NHS
work: The Attlee Government & the Welfare State (1945-1951)

front: Law: 1948 act, passed under Attlee, that abolished the last remnants of the workhouse system created by the 1834 Poor Law Amendment Act
work: The Attlee Government & the Welfare State (1945-1951)

front: Event: this party's 1950s period out of power, split between the left-wing Bevanites and moderate Gaitskellites, is nicknamed these "wasted" years
Figure: Minister of Labour under Attlee who resigned when funds for social programs were cut to pay for rearmament, lending his name to Labour's more radical wing in the 1950s
Event: in the 1959 election, the Conservatives urged voters not to let this party "ruin" their lives, and it lost
Document: portion of the Labour manifesto stating that workers should control the means of production; Hugh Gaitskell tried to alter it after the 1959 defeat
Figure: served non-consecutive terms as Labour PM in the 1960s and 70s, kept Britain out of the Vietnam War, and opposed Enoch Powell's "Rivers of Blood" speech
work: The Wilson-Callaghan Governments & the Winter of Discontent (1964-1979)

front: Event: Harold Wilson's government oversaw these massive reductions of the British railway network, despite opposition from his own party
work: The Wilson-Callaghan Governments & the Winter of Discontent (1964-1979)

front: Event: a Labour PM's controversial resignation honours list, which included peerages for businessmen thought to be corrupt
work: The Wilson-Callaghan Governments & the Winter of Discontent (1964-1979)

front: Event: this party legalized abortion under Home Secretary Roy Jenkins, per Tony Judt's Postwar
work: The Wilson-Callaghan Governments & the Winter of Discontent (1964-1979)

front: Event: James Callaghan's government collapsed after this 1978-79 wave of strikes, mocked by The Sun's "Crisis? What Crisis?" headline
work: The Wilson-Callaghan Governments & the Winter of Discontent (1964-1979)

front: Event: this party lost the 1979 general election to Margaret Thatcher's Conservatives, beginning nearly two decades out of power
work: The Wilson-Callaghan Governments & the Winter of Discontent (1964-1979)

front: Event: this party's period out of power from 1979 to 1997 is nicknamed these "years"

Document: this party's 1983 election manifesto, drafted principally by Michael Foot, was dubbed "the longest suicide note in history" by Gerald Kaufman and called for withdrawal from the European Economic Community
back: 1983 Labour Party manifesto ("The New Hope for Britain")

front: Figure: a moderate "Gang of Four" led by this politician broke away from Labour in the 1980s and founded the Social Democratic Party
Event: under Neil Kinnock, this party abandoned its largely symbolic Clause Four and defeated an attempted infiltration by the hard-left Militant Tendency
Event: this party lost the 1992 election to John Major's Conservatives partly due to the "War of Jennifer's Ear" advertising scandal
Figure: before the 1992 election, The Sun mocked this Labour leader with the "will the last person to leave Britain please turn out the lights" headline, then boasted "It's The Sun Wot Won It"
Institution: Tony Blair took this party in a "New" direction in the 1990s, adopting this centrist strategy named for a number
Institution: governed under Tony Blair and then Gordon Brown; David and Ed Miliband were both members during this era
Figure: became Labour leader in 2010 by defeating his brother David, who became Shadow Foreign Secretary; had formerly been Gordon Brown's Secretary of Energy and Climate Change
Figure: described in one clue as a "radical pacifist vegetarian," led Labour as part of a European left-wing surge alongside Greece's SYRIZA and Spain's Podemos
Event: this party's leader was removed amid accusations of fostering antisemitism and continued as an independent MP for Islington North
Institution: this politician took over the party's leadership from Jeremy Corbyn
Event: under Keir Starmer, this party won a landslide 2024 general election victory that removed Rishi Sunak's Conservative government
Event: despite its 2024 landslide, this party suffered its worst-ever result in the 2025 local elections, finishing fourth
Figure: this MP quit Labour after its leader was alleged to have abused corporate hospitality donations, having also clashed with him over the "For Women" case
Event: this Labour MP was forced to quit in March 2025 after being jailed for assaulting a constituent
Event: in 2024, this party's deputy leader was criticized after being filmed dancing in a nightclub to Gotye's "Somebody That I Used to Know"
Event: four independent candidates backed by "The Muslim Vote" defeated this party's members, including in the former seat of George Galloway, over its stance on Palestine
Event: this party refused to support Jamie Driscoll in a mayoral election after he appeared onstage with filmmaker Ken Loach, who had been expelled from it for joining "Labour Against the Witchhunt"
